title: Improvement Exchange Planning
name: Improvement Exchange Planning
metaTitle: Improvement Exchange Planning in San Antonio, TX
metaDescription: Improvement exchange planning for San Antonio 1031 transactions: using exchange funds for construction while an accommodation titleholder holds title.

sections.html:
An improvement exchange lets exchange funds pay for construction or renovation on the replacement property before the investor takes title, which matters in San Antonio when the ideal replacement site - vacant land near an I-35 distribution node, or a shell building needing buildout for medical office use - is not yet in a condition that matches the value exchanged out of. The structure relies on an exchange accommodation titleholder holding legal title while improvements are completed, all within the 180-day period.

This describes how the structure and its deadlines work, not tax advice; investors should confirm the strategy with their tax advisor before committing to a construction timeline inside the exchange period.

sections.heading: How an Exchange Accommodation Titleholder Holds Title During Construction
sections.html:
Because the investor cannot hold title to the replacement property before it reaches the value and condition needed for the exchange, an exchange accommodation titleholder - typically an entity set up for this purpose - takes and holds title while construction proceeds, using exchange funds directed through the qualified intermediary to pay contractors and suppliers. Title transfers to the investor only once the improvements are complete or the 180-day period is about to expire, whichever comes first.

The titleholder entity is generally established under the safe-harbor structure recognized for this purpose, with its role limited strictly to holding title and directing construction spending as instructed, rather than making independent decisions about the project.

sections.heading: The 180-Day Deadline for Completing and Transferring Improvements
sections.html:
Unlike a standard purchase, an improvement exchange has to fit both the acquisition of the underlying property and enough construction progress to justify the exchanged value within the same 180-day period that governs every other exchange. Improvements that are only partially complete at day 180 still transfer at whatever value has been added to that point - unfinished work does not count toward the exchange value, which is why realistic scheduling matters more here than in a standard forward exchange.

Building in a buffer between the projected construction completion date and day 180 gives the project room to absorb a permitting delay or a weather-related schedule slip without jeopardizing the exchange value that was planned for.

sections.heading: Identifying Improvement Work at the Outset
sections.html:
Because the property must be identified within 45 days along with a description specific enough to satisfy the unambiguous-description standard, planning typically nails down the following before day 45, so the identification notice describes a defined project rather than an open-ended construction budget:

  • The underlying parcel or shell structure being acquired
  • The scope of improvements the exchange funds will pay for
  • A contractor and permitting timeline that fits inside the remaining exchange period
  • A completion milestone that can be documented at day 180 if construction is not fully finished

sections.heading: San Antonio Construction Timelines and Improvement Exchange Risk
sections.html:
Permitting and construction timelines across San Antonio - particularly for buildout near the medical center corridor or industrial shell conversions along I-10 - can run longer than the 180-day exchange period allows if the scope is ambitious. Improvement exchange planning generally scales the construction scope to what can realistically be substantially complete within the deadline, rather than treating the exchange period as flexible once the project is underway.

Coordinating early with the city or county permitting office on a realistic timeline for the specific submarket and improvement type is one of the more useful steps an investor can take before committing to an improvement exchange strategy, since permitting delays are a common source of schedule slippage.

sections.heading: Coordinating Contractor Draws Through the Qualified Intermediary
sections.html:
Because the exchange accommodation titleholder rather than the investor is technically the party contracting for construction, draw requests from contractors and suppliers typically route through the qualified intermediary for approval and payment out of the held exchange funds, following a schedule tied to inspected work completed rather than a fixed calendar. That structure requires closer coordination than a standard construction loan draw process, since the QI has to confirm each disbursement fits within the exchange rules before releasing funds.

San Antonio investors planning an improvement exchange generally set the draw schedule and inspection checkpoints with the contractor and the QI before construction begins, so that funding does not stall mid-project waiting on documentation that should have been anticipated at the outset.

faqs.q: Who actually owns the replacement property while improvements are being built?
faqs.a: An exchange accommodation titleholder holds legal title during construction; the investor takes title once improvements are complete or the 180-day period is ending, whichever comes first.

faqs.q: What happens if construction is not finished by day 180?
faqs.a: The property transfers to the investor at whatever improved value exists at that point; only the completed value counts toward the exchange, and unfinished work does not extend the deadline.

faqs.q: Can raw land be the underlying property in an improvement exchange?
faqs.a: Yes, vacant land is a common underlying asset in an improvement exchange, particularly when the goal is to build out industrial or commercial space to match the exchanged value.

faqs.q: Does the 45-day identification deadline still apply to an improvement exchange?
faqs.a: Yes. The underlying property and the planned scope of improvements must be identified in writing within the same 45-day window that applies to any other exchange.

faqs.q: Is an improvement exchange more expensive to set up than a standard forward exchange?
faqs.a: Generally yes, because it requires an exchange accommodation titleholder entity and additional coordination with contractors and the qualified intermediary beyond a standard purchase closing.
